question: Define a finite set and an infinite set. Provide a clear example of each and explain why your examples satisfy the definitions.
expectedPoints: Definition of a finite set: a set whose elements can be put into a one-to-one correspondence with the set {1, 2, ..., n} for some natural number n.
Definition of an infinite set: a set that is not finite.
Clear, correct example of a finite set (e.g., The set of planets in our solar system).
Clear, correct example of an infinite set (e.g., The set of all natural numbers ℕ).
Explanation linking the examples back to the definitions.

question: Prove that the set of integers ℤ is an infinite set using the definition of a finite set (i.e., by showing it is not finite).
expectedPoints: Assume, for contradiction, that ℤ is finite.
State that if ℤ were finite, there would exist a bijection f: {1, 2, ..., n} → ℤ for some n.
Construct an integer (e.g., M = |f(1)| + |f(2)| + ... + |f(n)| + 1) that is not in the image of f.
Conclude that f cannot be surjective, contradicting the assumption that it is a bijection.
Therefore, ℤ cannot be finite and must be infinite.

question: Compare and contrast the cardinalities of finite sets and infinite sets. Discuss the concepts of countably infinite and uncountably infinite sets, providing a key example of each and explaining its significance in the context of metric spaces.
expectedPoints: Contrast: For finite sets, cardinality is a natural number; proper subsets have smaller cardinality. For infinite sets, a set can have the same cardinality as a proper subset (e.g., ℤ and ℕ).
Definition of countably infinite: a set with the same cardinality as the natural numbers ℕ.
Example of a countably infinite set relevant to metric spaces (e.g., ℚ, the rational numbers, is dense in ℝ).
Definition of uncountably infinite: an infinite set that is not countable.
Key example of an uncountably infinite set (e.g., ℝ, the real numbers, which is a complete metric space).
Significance: The distinction shows that not all infinities are the same and is fundamental to analysis (e.g., proving ℝ is not countable).
